analysis.note: Agrees with svabhāvasya; the singular denotes the doctrinal category whose members are exactly three, per the bhāṣya.

verification.detail: compound stem trividha as Pum/Sasthi/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Technical ‘own-nature/own-being’; the commentary defines it by seeming to exist through its own characteristic.

verification.detail: compound stem svabhāva as Pum/Sasthi/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Accusative singular feminine agreeing with niḥsvabhāvatām, the object of sandhāya.

verification.detail: compound stem trividha as Stri/Dvitiya/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Single abstract noun in -tā from niḥsvabhāva, not an independent tā; the bhāṣya treats trividhā niḥsvabhāvatā as a technical item.
Gerund/absolutive of sam-√dhā; here ‘having in view, intending, with reference to,’ not physical joining.
Genitive plural dependent on niḥsvabhāvatā: ‘the absence of own-nature of all dharmas.’

verification.detail: compound stem sarvadharma as Pum/Sasthi/Bahu: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Feminine nominative singular past passive participle from causative deśayati; agrees with niḥsvabhāvatā: ‘has been taught.’
Subject of deśitā; same technical abstract as the accusative earlier, here the generally taught thesis.

justifications.decision: The numerical expression is restrictive: there are three svabhāvas and no fourth.
options_considered: a technical triad of exactly three svabhāvas
a fourth or further svabhāva left implicit
justifications.chosen: trividhasya svabhāvasya = ‘of the threefold own-nature,’ i.e. the doctrinal triad of exactly three svabhāvas, not a loose adjective ‘various’ or an open-ended plurality.

evidence.quote: traya eva svabhāvā na caturtho 'stīti jñāpanārthaṃ saṃkhyānirdeśaḥ
evidence.translation: The numerical specification is for making known: there are just three svabhāvas; there is no fourth.

justifications.decision: Do not translate svabhāva as ordinary ‘character’ or as an unqualified ultimately real essence; the commentary defines it through seeming existence by its own lakṣaṇa.
options_considered: ultimately real essence asserted without qualification
own-nature/own-being as defined by seeming existence through its own characteristic
justifications.chosen: svabhāva is rendered ‘own-nature/own-being,’ with ‘own’ tied to a thing’s own characteristic and with vidyamānavat marking ‘as though existent.’

evidence.quote:
svena
svena lakṣaṇena vidyamānavad bhavatīti svbhāvaḥ
evidence.translation: It is svabhāva because it comes to be as though existent by its own, by its own characteristic.

justifications.decision: The first niḥsvabhāvatā is not a single undifferentiated negation; it has three specified modes.
options_considered: a generic statement that everything simply lacks essence
justifications.chosen: trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām = the threefold absence of own-nature: absence with respect to characteristic, arising, and ultimate reality.

evidence.quote:
trividhā niḥsvabhāvatā lakṣaṇaniḥsvabhāvatā utpattiniḥsvabhāvatā
paramārthaniḥsvabhāvatā ca
evidence.translation: The absence of own-nature is threefold: absence of own-nature with respect to characteristic, absence of own-nature with respect to arising, and absence of own-nature with respect to ultimate reality.

justifications.decision: The genitive does not mean ‘of all moral laws’ or ‘to all teachings’; the commentary identifies all dharmas by the Yogācāra three-nature scheme.
options_considered: all doctrines taught to an audience
all dharmas/phenomena structured by the three natures
justifications.chosen: sarvadharmāṇām is ‘of all dharmas/phenomena,’ where the domain is constituted by the imagined, dependent, and perfected natures.

evidence.translation: All dharmas are those whose nature consists of the imagined, the dependent, and the perfected.

justifications.decision: The universal teaching ‘the absence of own-nature of all dharmas was taught’ is to be read as made with reference to the threefold niḥsvabhāvatā of the three svabhāvas, not as a physical ‘joining’ or as an unrelated absolutist denial.
options_considered: ‘having in view / intending’ the preceding accusative
an unrestricted thesis of sheer nonexistence unrelated to the threefold scheme
justifications.chosen: sandhāya is construed with the preceding accusative trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām and rendered ‘having in view / with reference to.’

evidence.quote:
trividhā niḥsvabhāvatā lakṣaṇaniḥsvabhāvatā utpattiniḥsvabhāvatā
paramārthaniḥsvabhāvatā ca
evidence.translation: The absence of own-nature is threefold: absence with respect to characteristic, arising, and ultimate reality.
All dharmas are those whose nature consists of the imagined, the dependent, and the perfected.

translation:
Having in view the threefold absence of own-nature (niḥsvabhāvatā)
of the threefold own-nature (svabhāva),
the absence of own-nature of all dharmas (sarvadharmāḥ) was taught—
dharmas whose nature is imagined, dependent, and perfected.
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind translation might render trividhasya as merely ‘various’ or ‘triple’ and miss Sthiramati’s restriction: exactly three svabhāvas and no fourth.
It might flatten trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām into a single generic ‘essencelessness,’ missing the three modes: lakṣaṇa-, utpatti-, and paramārtha-niḥsvabhāvatā.
It might take sarvadharmāṇām as ‘all laws/teachings’ rather than all dharmas constituted by the imagined, dependent, and perfected natures.
It might translate sandhāya literally as ‘joining’ rather than ‘having in view / with reference to’ the threefold niḥsvabhāvatā.
analyzer_disagreements: For niḥsvabhāvatām and niḥsvabhāvatā, ByT5 segments niḥsvabhāva + tām/tā; I treat each as one abstract noun stem niḥsvabhāvatā with suffix -tā, since the verse has a single technical abstract and the bhāṣya at trbh 1394 says trividhā niḥsvabhāvatā.

For sarvadharmāṇām, ByT5 reports sarva_Cp plus inflected head dharma; per the requested compound convention, the lemma is the whole inflected compound stem sarvadharma. The bhāṣya re-quotes the unit as sarvadharmāḥ at trbh 1396.
For sandhāya, ByT5 gives the lexical compound verb saṃdhā; I normalize it as an indeclinable gerund of bare root dhā with prefix sam, as requested for verbal forms.
For deśitā, ByT5’s deśay lemma is normalized to the nominal participial stem deśita, feminine nominative singular, while retaining the causative-passive sense ‘taught.’
